elements.sent-2411-001: Graphene is a monolayer graphitic film where electrons behave like two-dimensional Dirac fermions without mass.
elements.sent-2411-002: Its study has attracted a wide interest in the domain of condensed matter physics.
elements.sent-2411-003: In particular, it represents an ideal system to test the comprehension of 2D massless relativistic particles in a laboratory, the Fermi velocity being $300$ times smaller than the speed of light.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we present a global well-posedness result for graphene in the Hartree-Fock approximation.
elements.sent-2411-005: The model allows to describe the time evolution of graphene in the presence of external electric fields, such as those induced by local defects in the monolayer of carbon atoms.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our approach is based on a well established non-perturbative framework originating from the study of three-dimensional quantum electrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-007: Graphene is a monolayer of carbon atoms packed into a dense honeycomb crystal structure.
elements.sent-2411-008: It is a two-dimensional allotrope of carbon whose comprehension can be considered as a first step for the understanding of electronic properties of other allotropes.
elements.sent-2411-009: Indeed, graphene can be seen as either an individual atomic plane extracted from graphite or an unrolled single-wall carbon nanotubes or a giant flat fullerene molecule.
elements.sent-2411-010: All these carbon-based systems show different structures, originating from the flexibility of their chemical bonds, with different physical properties depending mainly on the dimensionality of the structures themselves.
elements.sent-2411-011: Moreover, in condensed matter physics, most of the systems are described by the non-relativistic Schrödinger equation and their low energy excitations are quasi-particles with finite effective mass.
elements.sent-2411-012: On the contrary, electrons in graphene behave like two-dimensional Dirac fermions without mass.
elements.sent-2411-013: Indeed, this carbon allotrope has a quasi-particle dispersion relation which is conical at the degeneracy points, leading to an effective massless Dirac equation, where the speed of light is replaced by the Fermi velocity $v_\mathrm{F}$.
elements.sent-2411-014: These massless quasi-particles are proposed to be responsible for various anomalous phenomena observed in graphene \cite{GonGuiVoz-1996-PRL, NovGeiMorJia-2005-Nat, ZhaTanStoKim-2005-Nat .}
elements.sent-2411-015: For all these reasons, it has attracted a huge interest in the last decades \cite{CasGuiPerNov-2009-RMP .\par The main purpose of this paper is to present a global well-posedness result for the Cauchy problem describing the time evolution of graphene in the presence of external electrostatic fields, induced for instance by a set of local defects in the system.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The Fermi velocity in graphene being small (corresponding to a large value of the coupling constant), perturbation theory cannot be applied to this material.
elements.sent-2411-017: For this reason, we need to rely on non-perturbative methods such as mean-field approximations of Hartree-Fock type.
elements.sent-2411-018: As a consequence, this work is deeply inspired by \cite{HaiLewSpa-2012-JMP , where the authors study the Hartree-Fock approximation of graphene in an infinite volume with instantaneous Coulomb interactions, proving the existence of the ground state and deriving its properties.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This paper, in turn, represents an extension to 2D massless electrons of \cite{HaiLewSer-2005-CMP,HaiLewSer-2005-JPA,HaiLewSol-2007-CPAM , where the authors study the Hartree-Fock approximation of three-dimensional quantum electrodynamics (QED).}
elements.sent-2411-020: This model, named after Bogoliubov-Dirac-Fock (BDF), was suggested by a seminal paper of Chaix and Iracane \cite{ChaIra-1989-JPB and we refer the reader to \cite{EstLewSer-2008-BAMS,HaiLewSerSol-2007-PRA for a complete review.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The BDF framework is completely non-perturbative and holds for all values of the coupling constant $0\leq\alpha\leq 4/\pi\simeq 1.27$.
elements.sent-2411-022: For $\alpha>4/\pi$, the model is known to become unstable \cite{ChaIraLio-1989-JPB .\par Our two-dimensional theory contains ultraviolet (UV) divergences which represent a well known issue in quantum electrodynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Therefore, one needs to apply one of the well established regularization techniques (see for instance \cite{Lei-1975-RMP,PauVil-1949-RMP ) in order to overcome difficulties in the high frequencies domain.}
elements.sent-2411-024: It is known that these methods introduce some non-physical parameters which make unclear whether the obtained results are universal or depend on the chosen regularization technique.
elements.sent-2411-025: As a consequence, several procedures, known under the name of renormalization, have been studied in order to absorb the regularization parameters and get again a physical theory.
elements.sent-2411-026: In the present work, we impose a sharp ultraviolet cut-off $\Lambda>0$, as done for example in the three-dimensional electrostatic case \cite{HaiLewSer-2005-CMP,HaiLewSer-2005-JPA,HaiLewSol-2007-CPAM .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Because of the underlying lattice structure, this choice of the regularization method is natural for graphene.
elements.sent-2411-028: For this reason, we do not need to discuss the renormalization issue in our model.
elements.sent-2411-029: We mention that graphene has also been extensively studied in a series of papers, such as \cite{GiuMas-2009-PRB,GiuMas-2009-CMP,GiuMasPor-2010-PRB,GiuMasPor-2012-AP and related references, where rigorous lattice models are considered.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Moreover, we refer the reader to \cite{FefWei-2012-JAMS,FefWei-2013-CMP for the properties of honeycomb Schr\"odinger operators modeling graphene and similar materials.. \par {.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The paper is oganized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-032: In \cref{sec:hartree-fock-theory-of-graphene , the Hartree-Fock theory of graphene is introduced and discussed in detail, following \cite{HaiLewSpa-2012-JMP .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: It represents an extension to the two-dimensional case of the Bogoliubov-Dirac-Fock model, where the Dirac sea is replaced by the Fermi sea.
elements.sent-2411-034: Later, we consider the Cauchy problem for a BDF type evolution equation in which two-dimensional electrons interact through the instantaneous Coulomb potential and with a kinetic energy given by the massless Dirac operator.
elements.sent-2411-035: Then, we present the main result, stating the global well-posedness of the above-mentioned Cauchy problem.
elements.sent-2411-036: Finally, \cref{sec:global-well-posedness is devoted to the proof of the main result.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In particular, global existence is obtained thanks to an argument based on the conservation of energy and under the sole assumption that \begin{equation}0\leq\alpha\leq0.48637\quador, equivalently,\quad v_F\geq 2.0560,\end{equation} which covers the case of graphene, whose corresponding Fermi velocity is $v_\mathrm{F}\simeq 2.2$.

elements.section-2411-002: Hartree-Fock theory of graphene and main result
elements.sent-2411-039: We start by explaining how our model is derived from two-dimensional quantum electrodynamics, following \cite{HaiLewSpa-2012-JMP .}
elements.sent-2411-040: For the convenience of the reader, we then give its main properties, referring to that paper for more details and proofs.
elements.sent-2411-041: We also adopt notations from that reference.
elements.sent-2411-042: Electrons in graphene are essentially confined in 2D, but subjected to an electric field which acts in three spatial dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-043: For this reason we assume the interaction to be given by the 3D instantaneous Coulomb potential.
elements.sent-2411-044: Our starting point is the following formal 2D QED-type Hamiltonian, given in the Coulomb gauge by \begin{equation}\label{eq:HQED} \mathbb H^V=v_\mathrm{F}\int_{\R^2}\Psi^*\left(x\right)\,\mathbf{\sigma}\cdot\left(-i\nabla\right)\Psi\left(x\right)\,dx+\int_{\R^2}V\left(x\right)\rho\left(x\right)\,dx+\frac{1}{2}\iint_{\R^2\times\R^2}\frac{\rho\left(x\right)\rho\left(y\right)}{\abs{x-y}}\,dxdy\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-045: Notice that in our model photons are neglected.
elements.sent-2411-046: In \eqref{eq:HQED , $\boldsymbol{\sigma =\left(\sigma^1,\sigma^2\right)$, and $\sigma_1,\sigma_2$ are the first two Pauli matrices.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Here, and in what follows, we shall denote by \begin{equation}D^0=-i\mathbf{\sigma}\cdot\nabla=-i\sigma_1\partial_{x_1}-i\sigma_1\partial_{x_2}\end{equation} the massless 2D Dirac operator.
elements.sent-2411-048: The Hamiltonian acts on the fermionic Fock space $\mathscr{F}$.
elements.sent-2411-049: Notice that we work in atomic units, so that the mass and charge of the electrons are equal to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-050: The bare Fermi velocity appearing in \eqref{eq:HQED is about \begin{equation}v_F\simeq 2.20\end{equation} in graphene.}
elements.sent-2411-051: This shows that the kinetic part is of the same order of the last term of \eqref{eq:HQED and therefore we are not in a perturbative regime.}
elements.sent-2411-052: We point out that we do not consider the proper spin of electrons, since we do not include a magnetic field, but rather their pseudo-spin.
elements.sent-2411-053: The latter accounts for the presence of the two triangular lattices that compose the hexagonal structure of the graphene lattice.
elements.sent-2411-054: The Hamiltonian well describes the electrons with energies about the Fermi level, assuming they live on scale much larger that the lattice size, so that the continuum approximation leading to \eqref{eq:HQED is justified.}

elements.sent-2411-356: The main contribution of this work is the proof of the global well-posedness of the dynamics of a mean-field model for graphene, in which we consider external time-dependent electric fields, such as those induced by local charge defects in the monolayer of carbon atoms.\par This is achieved controlling the norm of the solution by means of the energy, which is not conserved since the external potential is non autonomous, but remains bounded over finite time intervals.\par We believe that this model can be seen as a first step in the study of scattering theory.
